sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: An ophthalmologist uses a slit-lamp biomicroscope to examine a patient's eye. The patient has a refractive error: distant objects are focused in front of the retina when the ciliary muscles are fully relaxed. The patient's uncorrected far point is 50 cm from the eye. The ophthalmologist prescribes corrective lenses to restore the far point to infinity.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hyperopia (farsightedness), because the focal point falls behind the retina

choices.label: B
choices.text: Myopia (nearsightedness), because parallel rays from distant objects converge before reaching the retina

choices.label: C
choices.text: Astigmatism, because the cornea has an irregular curvature

choices.label: D
choices.text: Presbyopia, because the lens has lost elasticity with age

explanations.A: In hyperopia, the eye has insufficient converging power, and the focal point would fall behind the retina. Distant objects would be blurred but the far point would be virtual (behind the eye), not at a finite distance in front. The passage states objects focus in front of the retina, which is myopia.
explanations.B: In myopia, the eye's optical system (cornea + lens) has too much converging power relative to the axial length of the eye. Parallel rays from distant objects are brought to a focus in front of the retina, producing a blurred image. The uncorrected far point is at a finite distance (50 cm here) rather than at infinity. Diverging (concave/negative) lenses are needed to move the focal point back onto the retina.
explanations.C: Astigmatism involves irregular corneal curvature producing different focal lengths in different meridians, causing directionally distorted images. The passage describes a simple focal length error with a well-defined far point, consistent with myopia, not astigmatism.
explanations.D: Presbyopia involves age-related loss of lens elasticity, reducing accommodation (near focusing ability). It affects the near point, not the far point. The passage describes a far-point defect.

questions.stem: The corrective lens needed for this patient should have a focal length of approximately:

choices.label: A
choices.text: −25 cm, because the corrective power must be twice the refractive error

choices.label: B
choices.text: +50 cm (converging lens), because the lens must add converging power to focus on the retina

choices.label: C
choices.text: −50 cm (diverging lens), because the lens must create a virtual image at the patient's far point for objects at infinity

choices.label: D
choices.text: +100 cm, because a weak converging lens will shift the focal point forward

explanations.A: The focal length is determined directly by the far point distance, not by doubling. f = −50 cm, not −25 cm.
explanations.B: A converging lens would add more focusing power, making the myopia worse by moving the focal point even further in front of the retina. Myopia requires a diverging lens to spread rays before they enter the eye.
explanations.C: The corrective lens must take an object at infinity (parallel rays) and create a virtual image at the patient's far point (50 cm). Using the thin lens equation with object distance = infinity and image distance = −50 cm (virtual, same side as object): 1/f = 1/∞ + 1/(−50) = −1/50. So f = −50 cm = −0.5 m, giving a power of −2.0 diopters. The negative focal length indicates a diverging (concave) lens.
explanations.D: A converging lens of any power would worsen myopia. The correction requires a diverging lens with negative focal length.

questions.stem: When the ophthalmologist shines a narrow beam of white light through a prism to examine the patient's retina, the light separates into its component colors. The violet light bends more than the red light because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: violet light has a shorter wavelength, which corresponds to a higher index of refraction in the glass, producing greater bending according to Snell's law

choices.label: B
choices.text: violet light has a higher amplitude, which increases the force of refraction at the glass surface

choices.label: C
choices.text: red light is absorbed more strongly by the glass, leaving violet to emerge at a steeper angle

choices.label: D
choices.text: violet light travels faster in glass than red light, causing it to bend more at the interface

explanations.A: Dispersion occurs because the index of refraction of glass varies with wavelength — shorter wavelengths (violet, ~400 nm) experience a higher index of refraction than longer wavelengths (red, ~700 nm). By Snell's law (n₁sinθ₁ = n₂sinθ₂), a higher n₂ produces a greater change in angle upon entering the glass. This wavelength-dependent refraction separates white light into its spectrum.
explanations.B: Amplitude determines the intensity (brightness) of light, not its degree of refraction. Refraction depends on the change in wave speed between media, which is wavelength-dependent, not amplitude-dependent.
explanations.C: While some absorption does occur, the separation of colors in a prism is due to differential refraction, not differential absorption. Both red and violet light are transmitted through the glass.
explanations.D: Violet light travels slower in glass than red light (higher index = slower speed = v = c/n). A higher index means more bending, not faster travel. This answer reverses the relationship between speed and refraction.

questions.passage: A radiologist reviews a patient's chest X-ray. The X-ray tube operates at a voltage of 120 kVp, producing a spectrum of X-ray photons with maximum energy of 120 keV. The radiologist notes that bone appears white (radiopaque), soft tissue appears gray, and air-filled lungs appear dark (radiolucent). A lead apron with a thickness of 0.5 mm is used to shield the patient's reproductive organs during the procedure.
questions.stem: X-ray photons with a maximum energy of 120 keV correspond to a minimum wavelength of approximately (h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, 1 eV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J):

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1.03 × 10⁻¹⁴ m, which falls in the gamma ray range

choices.label: B
choices.text: 1.03 × 10⁻⁸ m, which falls in the ultraviolet range

choices.label: C
choices.text: 5.5 × 10⁻⁷ m, which falls in the visible light range

choices.label: D
choices.text: 1.03 × 10⁻¹¹ m, calculated from λ = hc/E using the maximum photon energy

explanations.A: A wavelength of 10⁻¹⁴ m is nuclear-scale and far shorter than diagnostic X-ray wavelengths. It would correspond to a photon energy in the MeV range (roughly 100 MeV), not 120 keV. Diagnostic X-rays have wavelengths on the order of 10⁻¹¹ m (0.01 nm).
explanations.B: 10⁻⁸ m = 10 nm, which is in the extreme UV / soft X-ray boundary. This is three orders of magnitude too large for 120 keV photons.
explanations.C: 5.5 × 10⁻⁷ m = 550 nm, which is visible green light. Visible photons have energies of ~2–3 eV, not 120,000 eV.
explanations.D: E = 120 keV = 120,000 × 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ = 1.92 × 10⁻¹⁴ J. λ = hc/E = (6.63 × 10⁻³⁴)(3 × 10⁸) / (1.92 × 10⁻¹⁴) = 1.99 × 10⁻²⁵ / 1.92 × 10⁻¹⁴ ≈ 1.03 × 10⁻¹¹ m = 0.0103 nm. This is squarely in the X-ray range (0.01–10 nm). The minimum wavelength corresponds to the maximum energy photon.

questions.stem: Bone appears white on an X-ray because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: bone emits visible white light when struck by X-ray photons

choices.label: B
choices.text: bone has a low atomic number and is transparent to X-rays

choices.label: C
choices.text: calcium and phosphorus in bone have high atomic numbers relative to soft tissue, causing greater X-ray absorption and less transmission to the detector

choices.label: D
choices.text: bone reflects X-rays back toward the X-ray tube, creating a bright spot on the image

explanations.A: Bone does not emit visible light in response to X-rays. The white appearance on an X-ray film or detector represents areas where fewer X-ray photons reached the detector because they were absorbed by the dense material.
explanations.B: This is the opposite of what happens. Bone is radiopaque (absorbs X-rays) because of its high-Z components. Low atomic number materials like air are radiolucent (transparent to X-rays).
explanations.C: X-ray absorption increases with atomic number (approximately proportional to Z³ for photoelectric absorption). Calcium (Z=20) and phosphorus (Z=15) in hydroxyapatite absorb X-rays much more effectively than the carbon (Z=6), hydrogen (Z=1), nitrogen (Z=7), and oxygen (Z=8) that compose soft tissue. Fewer photons pass through bone, leaving that region unexposed (white) on the detector.
explanations.D: X-rays are not reflected by bone. They are either absorbed (photoelectric effect) or scattered (Compton scattering). The image is formed by differential transmission, not reflection.

questions.passage: A physics student sets up a double-slit experiment using monochromatic green laser light (λ = 532 nm). The two slits are separated by 0.20 mm, and an interference pattern is observed on a screen 2.0 m away. The student measures the distance from the central maximum to the first-order bright fringe.
questions.stem: The distance from the central maximum to the first-order bright fringe is approximately:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 53 mm, because double-slit fringes are always widely spaced

choices.label: B
choices.text: 5.3 cm, because the fringe spacing is proportional to L/λ

choices.label: C
choices.text: 0.53 mm, because the fringe spacing equals the slit separation

explanations.A: 53 mm is off by a factor of 10 from the correct value. Fringe spacing depends on the specific experimental parameters, not a general rule about double-slit experiments.
explanations.B: 5.3 cm is off by a factor of 10. The calculation gives 5.3 mm. Additionally, the stated formula (L/λ) is inverted — fringe spacing is proportional to λL/d, not L/λ.
explanations.C: The fringe spacing depends on λ, L, and d — it does not simply equal the slit separation. With λ = 532 nm, L = 2 m, and d = 0.2 mm, the spacing is much larger than 0.53 mm.
explanations.D: For double-slit interference, the position of the mth bright fringe is y = mλL/d. For m=1: y = (1)(532 × 10⁻⁹ m)(2.0 m) / (0.20 × 10⁻³ m) = 1.064 × 10⁻⁶ / 2.0 × 10⁻⁴ = 5.32 × 10⁻³ m = 5.3 mm. The small angle approximation (sinθ ≈ tanθ ≈ y/L) is valid because y << L.

questions.stem: If the student replaces the green laser (532 nm) with a red laser (635 nm) while keeping all other parameters constant, the interference pattern will:

choices.label: A
choices.text: remain unchanged because interference depends only on slit separation, not wavelength

choices.label: B
choices.text: have more closely spaced fringes because red light has a longer wavelength

choices.label: C
choices.text: have more widely spaced fringes because fringe spacing is proportional to wavelength

choices.label: D
choices.text: disappear because red light cannot produce interference patterns

explanations.A: Fringe spacing y = λL/d is directly proportional to wavelength. Changing from 532 nm to 635 nm will change the pattern.
explanations.B: This reverses the relationship. Fringe spacing is proportional to λ, so a longer wavelength produces wider spacing, not narrower.
explanations.C: Since y = λL/d, increasing λ from 532 nm to 635 nm increases the fringe spacing by a factor of 635/532 ≈ 1.19. The fringes become about 19% more widely spaced. This is consistent with the general principle that longer wavelengths diffract more.
explanations.D: All wavelengths of light can produce interference patterns. Red light, like all coherent monochromatic light, produces clear double-slit interference fringes.

questions.stem: Total internal reflection occurs when light travels from a medium with a higher index of refraction to one with a lower index and the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle. If light travels from glass (n = 1.50) to water (n = 1.33), the critical angle is approximately:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 62.5°, calculated from sinθc = n₂/n₁ = 1.33/1.50 = 0.887

choices.label: B
choices.text: 41.8°, calculated from sinθc = n₁/n₂ = 1.50/1.33

choices.label: C
choices.text: 90°, because total internal reflection can only occur at the glass-air interface

choices.label: D
choices.text: 48.8°, calculated from sinθc = 1/n₁ = 1/1.50

explanations.A: The critical angle is found from Snell's law at the point where the refracted angle equals 90°: n₁sinθc = n₂sin90° → sinθc = n₂/n₁ = 1.33/1.50 = 0.887 → θc = arcsin(0.887) ≈ 62.5°. For angles of incidence greater than 62.5°, light will undergo total internal reflection at the glass-water interface.
explanations.B: This inverts the ratio. sinθc = n₂/n₁ (lower index over higher index), not n₁/n₂. Using n₁/n₂ = 1.50/1.33 = 1.128 gives sinθc > 1, which is impossible.
explanations.C: Total internal reflection can occur at any interface where light passes from a higher-n to a lower-n medium, not just glass-air. The glass-water interface supports total internal reflection because n_glass > n_water. The critical angle depends on the specific indices of both media.
explanations.D: sinθc = 1/n₁ gives the critical angle for a glass-air interface (n₂ = 1.00), not a glass-water interface. For glass-water, sinθc = 1.33/1.50, which gives a larger critical angle than 1/1.50 would.

questions.stem: A photon of ultraviolet light has a wavelength of 250 nm. Compared to a photon of infrared light with a wavelength of 1000 nm, the UV photon has:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 4 times the energy but the same frequency, because frequency is a property of the source, not the photon

choices.label: B
choices.text: 1/4 the energy, because longer wavelengths carry more energy

choices.label: C
choices.text: the same energy but higher frequency, because all photons carry the same quantum of energy

choices.label: D
choices.text: 4 times the energy and 4 times the frequency, because E = hf = hc/λ and both are inversely proportional to wavelength

explanations.A: Frequency and energy are both properties of the photon, and they are proportional (E = hf). A photon with 4× the energy necessarily has 4× the frequency. The frequency is determined by the wavelength, not independently by the source.
explanations.B: Energy is inversely proportional to wavelength: E = hc/λ. Shorter wavelengths carry more energy, not less. This is why UV radiation is more biologically damaging than IR.
explanations.C: Different photons carry different amounts of energy depending on their frequency. The quantum of energy is hf, which varies with f. All photons of the same frequency carry the same energy, but photons of different frequencies do not.
explanations.D: E = hf = hc/λ. The UV photon (250 nm) has a wavelength 4 times shorter than the IR photon (1000 nm), so it has 4 times the energy and 4 times the frequency. Since f = c/λ, halving the wavelength doubles the frequency, and quartering it quadruples the frequency. Energy and frequency are directly proportional (E = hf) and both inversely proportional to wavelength.

questions.stem: A student places an object 15 cm in front of a converging (convex) lens with a focal length of 10 cm. The image formed is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: real, inverted, and located 30 cm on the opposite side of the lens

choices.label: B
choices.text: virtual, upright, and located 30 cm on the same side as the object

choices.label: C
choices.text: real, inverted, and located 6 cm on the opposite side of the lens

choices.label: D
choices.text: virtual, upright, and located 6 cm on the same side as the object

explanations.A: Using the thin lens equation: 1/f = 1/do + 1/di → 1/10 = 1/15 + 1/di → 1/di = 1/10 − 1/15 = 3/30 − 2/30 = 1/30 → di = +30 cm. The positive image distance indicates a real image on the opposite side of the lens. Magnification m = −di/do = −30/15 = −2, meaning the image is inverted and twice the size of the object.
explanations.B: A virtual image at 30 cm would require a negative di, but the calculation gives di = +30 cm (real). Virtual, upright images from converging lenses only occur when the object is inside the focal length (do < f).
explanations.C: di = 6 cm would require 1/6 = 1/10 − 1/15 = 1/30, which is incorrect. 1/6 ≠ 1/30.
explanations.D: The object is outside the focal length (15 cm > 10 cm), so a converging lens produces a real, inverted image, not a virtual, upright one.

questions.stem: An endoscope uses fiber optic cables to transmit light into the body. Light is guided through the fiber by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: diffraction of light around the curved fiber walls

choices.label: B
choices.text: total internal reflection at the interface between the high-index glass core and the low-index cladding

choices.label: C
choices.text: constructive interference of light waves within the fiber

choices.label: D
choices.text: polarization of light along the fiber axis, preventing it from escaping through the walls

explanations.A: Diffraction involves the bending of waves around obstacles or through apertures. Light guidance in fiber optics relies on reflection, not diffraction. Diffraction would cause light to spread out, not remain confined.
explanations.B: Fiber optic cables have a high-index glass core surrounded by a lower-index cladding. When light enters the core at a sufficiently steep angle (greater than the critical angle at the core-cladding interface), it undergoes total internal reflection at each bounce and propagates along the fiber with minimal loss. This principle allows endoscopes to transmit images and illumination through curved paths inside the body.
explanations.C: While interference effects can occur in specialized fibers (e.g., photonic crystal fibers), standard fiber optic light guidance is based on total internal reflection, not constructive interference.
explanations.D: Polarization describes the orientation of the electric field oscillation. Standard fiber optics do not rely on polarization for light confinement. Both polarization states propagate through standard multimode fibers.

questions.stem: The intensity of electromagnetic radiation from a point source decreases with distance according to the inverse square law. If a light detector measures an intensity of 400 W/m² at a distance of 2 m from a point source, the intensity at 4 m from the source is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 200 W/m², because intensity decreases linearly with distance

choices.label: B
choices.text: 100 W/m², because intensity is inversely proportional to the square of the distance

choices.label: C
choices.text: 800 W/m², because intensity increases with distance from a point source

choices.label: D
choices.text: 50 W/m², because the intensity drops by a factor of 8 when distance doubles

explanations.A: The inverse square law states I ∝ 1/r², not I ∝ 1/r. Linear decrease would be an inverse (first power) law, not an inverse square law. Doubling the distance reduces intensity by a factor of 4, not 2.
explanations.B: By the inverse square law: I₁/I₂ = r₂²/r₁². With r₁ = 2 m and r₂ = 4 m: I₂ = I₁ × (r₁/r₂)² = 400 × (2/4)² = 400 × 1/4 = 100 W/m². The energy from a point source spreads over the surface area of a sphere (4πr²), so doubling the radius quadruples the area and reduces the intensity to one-quarter.
explanations.C: Intensity decreases with distance from a point source, not increases. Energy conservation requires that the same total power is distributed over an ever-larger spherical area.
explanations.D: Doubling the distance reduces intensity by a factor of 2² = 4, not 8. A factor of 8 would apply if intensity followed an inverse cube law, which electromagnetic radiation from a point source does not.

questions.stem: A polarizing filter transmits only light whose electric field oscillates along the filter's transmission axis. Unpolarized light of intensity I₀ passes through two polarizing filters in sequence. The first filter's axis is vertical, and the second is oriented at 45° to the vertical. The intensity of light emerging from the second filter is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: I₀/4, because the first filter halves the intensity and the second reduces it by cos²45° = 1/2

choices.label: B
choices.text: I₀/2, because only the second filter affects the intensity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Zero, because the two filters are not aligned

choices.label: D
choices.text: I₀, because polarized light passes through any polarizing filter without loss

explanations.A: After the first polarizer, unpolarized light of intensity I₀ is reduced to I₀/2 (only the component aligned with the vertical axis passes). The now vertically polarized light hits the second filter at 45°. By Malus's law, the transmitted intensity is (I₀/2)cos²45° = (I₀/2)(1/2) = I₀/4. The total transmitted intensity is one-quarter of the original.
explanations.B: Both filters affect the intensity. The first filter halves the intensity of unpolarized light (from I₀ to I₀/2). The second filter further reduces it according to Malus's law.
explanations.C: Zero transmission occurs only when the two filters are perpendicular (90° apart). At 45°, cos²45° = 0.5, so half the polarized light from the first filter passes through the second.
explanations.D: Polarized light loses intensity when passing through a polarizer oriented at any angle other than 0° to its polarization direction. Malus's law (I = I₀cos²θ) governs the transmitted intensity.

questions.stem: Which region of the electromagnetic spectrum has wavelengths immediately shorter than visible light and is responsible for causing sunburn and DNA damage in skin cells?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Infrared radiation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Microwave radiation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ultraviolet radiation

choices.label: D
choices.text: X-ray radiation

explanations.A: Infrared radiation has wavelengths longer than visible light (>700 nm), not shorter. It is associated with thermal energy and heat sensation, not sunburn or DNA damage.
explanations.B: Microwave radiation has wavelengths much longer than visible light (millimeters to centimeters). It is used in communication and microwave ovens and does not cause sunburn.
explanations.C: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation occupies the electromagnetic spectrum immediately below visible light (wavelengths ~10–400 nm). UV-B (280–315 nm) and UV-A (315–400 nm) penetrate the skin and can cause direct DNA damage through formation of pyrimidine dimers (particularly thymine dimers). This DNA damage triggers the inflammatory response perceived as sunburn and, if unrepaired, can lead to mutations and skin cancer.
explanations.D: X-rays have wavelengths shorter than UV (0.01–10 nm) and are not immediately adjacent to visible light. While X-rays can damage DNA, they are not the primary cause of sunburn from solar radiation because most X-rays from the sun are absorbed by the atmosphere.

questions.stem: A concave (converging) mirror has a focal length of 20 cm. An object is placed 10 cm in front of the mirror (inside the focal point). The image formed is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: real, inverted, and located 20 cm in front of the mirror

choices.label: B
choices.text: virtual, upright, and located 20 cm behind the mirror

choices.label: C
choices.text: real, inverted, and located at the focal point

choices.label: D
choices.text: no image is formed when the object is inside the focal point of a concave mirror

explanations.A: For an object inside the focal point of a concave mirror, the reflected rays diverge and do not form a real image in front of the mirror. A real, inverted image is formed only when the object is outside the focal point.
explanations.B: Using the mirror equation: 1/f = 1/do + 1/di → 1/20 = 1/10 + 1/di → 1/di = 1/20 − 1/10 = 1/20 − 2/20 = −1/20 → di = −20 cm. The negative image distance indicates a virtual image located 20 cm behind the mirror. Magnification m = −di/do = −(−20)/10 = +2, so the image is upright and twice the size of the object. This is the principle behind magnifying/shaving mirrors.
explanations.C: The image is not at the focal point. The focal point is where parallel rays converge, not where an image forms for an object at 10 cm. The mirror equation gives di = −20 cm (behind the mirror).
explanations.D: An image is always formed — when the object is inside the focal point, the reflected rays diverge, but they appear to originate from a point behind the mirror, forming a virtual image.
